item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think wellstone got little sentimental during the audio transcript. Usually interviewers tend to not show a lot of emotion in scp articles unless something really fucked is going on. I'm not the best at giving critique so I apologize if this wasn't verry helpful

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah no, this is is how I wanted it to be read as.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: as with the above comments, there's still quite a bit of proofreading needed, and as such, i'm going to -1 it for now. the tone and grammar weirdness in this article is just too distracting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's the image or the brevity or a combo of both, but this has a really dreamlike feel about it that I really enjoyed. The dialogue as well, it was realistic but still subtle.

Nice post +1

However, don't you think this should just be Safe class?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wellstone talks in favor to life and the importance of it to a person who probably got there by being taken away from death row, who probably killed people for petty (or not so petty) reasons.

What's happening to the D-class goes well beyond whatever he's done to end up here. He's talking about what he'll be remembered for and how futile it is to fix or change anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi! Thanks for posting to the site!

After reading it, I have decided on no-vote for now, as I've identified some tone errors and word choice issues.

+ Note 1
- Note 1

Beyond the assigned research and containment staff chosen, no other personnel are to enter this area of exclusion. Any individuals or personnel that do are to be detained and brought in for questioning. Detained individuals are to undergo therapeutic treatment and amnesticized from ever encountering SCP-4622-1.

This section reads a little bit weird, specifically the "…and amnesticized from ever encountering…". People usually aren't "amnesticized from" anything. It might be easier to say:

Suggestion:

No other personnel are authorized to enter the exclusion zone. Violators are to be detained for questioning then amnesticized. Psychotherapy is then to be administered, as needed.

A lot of clinical tone comes from being as clear and concise as possible i.e. use as few words to say what you have to say.

+ Note 2
- Note 2

Description: SCP-4622 is an unmarked and open grave in front of a tree near the peak of a hill located in the Chugach Mountains of Alaska. A tombstone is affixed to the grave but bears no name or identity of the figure lying within.

Even though it isn't, the first sentence here reads reads like a run-on.
"An unmarked and open grave…
IN FRONT OF a tree….
NEAR the peak…
OF A hill…
LOCATED IN the Chugach Mountains
OF Alaska."

Suggestion:

Description: SCP-4622 is the designation for an anomalous open gravesite in the Chugach Mountains of Alaska. A tombstone is present, but bears no name or other markings.

Because it's so early in the article, you want to have things to draw the reader in. If you do all the work for them, they might not like it.

+ Note 3
- Note 3

The figure, hereafter referred to as SCP-4622-1, is a corpse in an advanced state of decomposition that is heavily distorted and warped beyond human perception. Further observation of SCP-4622-1 has been known to cause mild eyestrain, headaches, and nosebleeds in the observing individual. SCP-4622-1 carries another anomalous effect: observers will perceive the figure in the grave as themselves and will attempt to fill it with soil from SCP-4622-2.

A segment that bothers me a lot is "heavily distorted and warped beyond human perception." Distorted in what way? Has the body physically changed? What does "beyond human perception" mean? Can we not perceive it? Imagine a researcher who isn't tasked to this anomaly reading this report; I don't think they'd get very much out that sentence.

We also really don't need to designate every little thing as an SCP-4622-#, even if those things are anomalous. If they're never seprable from the actual anomaly (the gravesite), designating them with their own numbers just serves to clutter the article. That said, the body might still deserve it's own designation, but the pile of soil definitely does not.

Suggestion:

An unidentified human corpse, designated SCP-4622-1, is present within the grave. The body is in an advanced state of decomposition. When viewed by an observer SCP-4622-1 exhibits mild cognitohazardous effects, inducing eyestrain, headaches, and nosebleeds. This is secondary to its primary anomalous effect: observers will perceive the body as their own and attempt to fill the grave using the surrounding soil.

+ Note 4
- Note 4

SCP-4622-2 is a mound of soil with a shovel affixed to it, located to the right, beside the grave. SCP-4622-2 instantly replenishes any of its mass lost when not observed. Likewise, any attempt to fill SCP-4622 will be unsuccessful as no matter how much soil has been shoveled into the grave, SCP-4622 will always remain unchanged.

Following what was said above in Note 3, the soil mound doesn't need its own designation.

Suggestion:

When unobserved, any progress made on filling the grave will be anomalously reverted back to its undisturbed state.

It's getting late and I'm sorry I do not have notes for you regarding the dialogue portion.

Thank you again for posting. I hope this feedback is more helpful than not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So if I got this right then this is a grave and a tree on a field that make you have an existential crisis? I love it!

The only thing that pulled me away from the typical SCP feel (although this might have been intentional) is the way that Dr. Wallstone talks to D-53. It feels to me more like they have a deeper bond than just "scientist" and "subject" if Dr. Wallstone talks to him this way.

+ Show examples
- Hide examples

Dr. Wallstone: Hello? D-53? Robert, can you hear me?

He calls him by his first name which indicates that they are closer to each other (I'm not even sure whether D-Class personell even have names.)

Dr. Wallstone: It's part of the test. Just do it.

Which for a scientist who is audio-logging all of this sounds a little unprofessional. It would be more convincing if he at least called it an "experiment".

There's only one little thing left which I'm not sure whether it is correct or not but:

+ Show block
- Hide block

Detained individuals are to undergo therapeutic treatment and amnesticized from ever encountering SCP-4622-1.

If you remove the "undergo therapeutic treatment and"
then you get:

Detained individuals are to amnesticized from ever encountering SCP-4622-1.

Which is an incorrect sentence. As I said I'm not sure if this is allowed in english but I would definitely write it like this:

Detained individuals are to undergo therapeutic treatment and be amnesticized from ever encountering SCP-4622-1.

I hope this was useful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Taking th article at face value, albeit emotionally compelling, it lacked in further developing the D-Class. Wellstone talks in favor to life and the importance of it to a person who probably got there by being taken away from death row, who probably killed people for petty (or not so petty) reasons. By ignoring that mere fact, both D53 and wellstone's perspective lost my suspension of desbelief. Even after the spoiler collapsible, I still consider you could have gotten your original message accross with this. Novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Got it. The reason I asked/mulled it over is because the nature of his dialogue changes a bit toward the end, though that has a perfectly non-anomalous explanation of the D-Class's behavior getting under his skin.

Really well done, regardless!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's not affected by it. You're only affected if you look in the grave, which is why the entire log is just audio and no video.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, "damn" is right. Really good work with making the dialogue naturalistic and getting across so much emotion without needing to spell it out.

Made a couple very minor edits.

stray thought:

+ show block
– hide block

Is Wallstone being affected by the anomaly during the conversation? I'm not sure whether it matters necessarily or whether I'd change anything either way, but it's something I'm mulling over as I think back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: unsuccessful would likely work better than unattainable?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed that last part. I changed the wording of that sentence as well as the "impossible" thing into something else. If it doesn't work, feel free to revert the change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
damn. i know i don't fully get this but still. damn.

+ minor things
- minor things

Likewise, any soil used to fill the hole of SCP-4622-1 will be impossible as no matter how much soil has been shoveled into the grave, SCP-4622 will always remain unchanged.

that impossible is very out of place and i'm not sure what the best way to fix this is

task force personnel apprehended D-53040 from SCP-4622

that from feels off; i think "at" might work better?

(also feel free to revert my change of a bunch of the remaining SCP-4685's to SCP-4622 if that was a deliberate thing)

item.title: coldpost
item.content:encoded:
To die, to sleep-
No more- and by a sleep to say we end
The heartache and the thousand natural shocks
That flesh is heir to-’tis a consummation
Devoutly to be wished! To die, to sleep.
To sleep, perchance to dream-ay, there’s the rub,
For in that sleep of death what dreams may come
When we have shuffled off this mortal coil,
must give us pause. There’s the respect that makes calamity of so long life.

- William Shakespeare, Hamlet, Act III, Scene I

And he took bread, gave thanks and broke it, and gave it to them, saying, "This is my body given for you; do this in remembrance of me."

- Luke 22:19, NIV

Before we jump into this, yes, this is a very trite and self-indulgent coldpost that offers very little story elements (there is a small bit of story re: the scip though), but I just needed to get this off of my chest. There, I said it so you don't have to bring it up in your criticisms again. I'm well aware.

spoilery rant stuff
eh

So… if you're wondering what this is about, out-of-universe, this article represents how I deal with relationships.

No, not intimate relationships. Just relationships in general. Both online and IRL.

This originally started as an idea about what happens to us when we die, but then stuff happened and I just added that stuff to here and it snowballed into this thing.

You won't find a clear story about what it's all about, it's all meant to be metaphorical and general theorizing is pretty much all you've got to go on here. There is at least one hint re: what the anomaly actually is, but I'm not revealing too much.

SOURCE: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Matheran_view_point_lone_tree.jpg
